category_summary:
A landmark cultural moment dominated discussions: an AI-generated story [won the **Commonwealth Prize**](/?date=2026-05-19&category=social#item-99b918e45a5a) for the Caribbean region, fooling literary judges—a genuine Turing Test milestone for creative AI.

- **Anthropic** [announced its acquisition](/?date=2026-05-19&category=social#item-7609f18610d9) of **Stainless API**, signaling major investment in SDK and MCP ecosystem infrastructure
- **NVIDIA** [delivered its first custom CPU](/?date=2026-05-19&category=social#item-cda7be962867) (**Vera**) to **Anthropic**, **OpenAI**, **SpaceX**, and **Oracle**—marking its expansion beyond GPUs into agentic AI hardware
- **Nathan Lambert** [identified on-policy distillation](/?date=2026-05-19&category=social#item-a8b8c6acab2d) as an emerging canonical post-training method alongside SFT, RLHF, and DPO
- **Nous Research** [published Token Superposition Training](/?date=2026-05-19&category=social#item-f76ffd9df2b6), a novel method claiming 2-3x faster LLM training without changing the final model
- **François Chollet** [offered a widely-shared mental model](/?date=2026-05-19&category=social#item-4cb0ee613ede) for coding agents: place verifiable constraints (walls) so blind exploration converges on correct outputs

Practical AI deployment milestones also drew attention: **Sam Altman** [highlighted 1 billion images](/?date=2026-05-19&category=social#item-407d328cd04f) generated in India via ChatGPT Images 2.0, **Antirez** (Redis creator) [demonstrated running **DeepSeek V4 Flash**](/?date=2026-05-19&category=social#item-a6479e16e0ca) (284B params) locally on a MacBook, and **Greg Brockman** [revealed Codex's `/goal` feature](/?date=2026-05-19&category=social#item-82d7b5357828) for autonomous agentic workflows.

items.title: On-policy distillation is on track to be a lasting method in post-training. The list of areas would ...
items.content:
On-policy distillation is on track to be a lasting method in post-training. The list of areas would be:

Instruction tuning (SFT/IFT)
RLHF
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO et al)
RLVR
On-policy Distillation (OPD)

New classes of methods are rare! Excited to play.

items.title:
What if you could train LLMs 2-3x faster without changing the final model at all? 

Most of that mon...
items.content:
What if you could train LLMs 2-3x faster without changing the final model at all? 

Most of that money goes into processing one token at a time, billions of times over.
 
Nous Research published a paper introducing Token Superposition Training.
 
It's a drop-in method that cuts pretraining time by up to 2.5x.
 
Here's how it works:
 
> Group contiguous tokens into bags
> Average their embeddings together
> Predict the next bag jointly
> Use multi-hot cross-entropy as loss
> Revert to normal training midway
 
The switch happens after the first 20-40% of training.
 
From there, it finishes with standard next-token prediction.
 
The final weights match a conventionally trained model.
 
No changes to the optimizer, tokenizer, data, or architecture are required.
 
The team tested it at 270M, 600M, 3B, and a 10B mixture-of-experts scale.
 
It consistently beats baseline loss and downstream evals.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: Nous Research published a paper on Token Superposition Training - a method that groups tokens into bags, averages embeddings, and uses multi-hot cross-entropy loss for first 20-40% of training before reverting to standard next-token prediction. Claims 2-2.5x pretraining speedup with equivalent final model quality.
items.reasoning: Novel training efficiency technique from Nous Research with significant practical implications. Tested at scale up to 10B MoE. The drop-in nature and no architecture changes make this highly practical. Moderate engagement but strong technical content.
items.summary_html: Nous Research published a paper on Token Superposition Training - a method that groups tokens into bags, averages embeddings, and uses multi-hot cross-entropy loss for first 20-40% of training before reverting to standard next-token prediction. Claims 2-2.5x pretraining speedup with equivalent final model quality.
items.content_html:

It's a drop-in method that cuts pretraining time by up to 2.5x.

Here's how it works:

> Group contiguous tokens into bags

> Average their embeddings together

> Predict the next bag jointly

> Use multi-hot cross-entropy as loss

> Revert to normal training midway

The switch happens after the first 20-40% of training.

From there, it finishes with standard next-token prediction.

The final weights match a conventionally trained model.

No changes to the optimizer, tokenizer, data, or architecture are required.

The team tested it at 270M, 600M, 3B, and a 10B mixture-of-experts scale.

items.title:
/1 Garry Tan tests a 1M token coding model running locally on a MacBook Pro.

Antirez, the person wh...
items.content:
/1 Garry Tan tests a 1M token coding model running locally on a MacBook Pro.

Antirez, the person who built Redis, just shipped ds4: a custom engine that runs DeepSeek V4 Flash, a massive 284B parameter AI model, fully on your MacBook. No cloud. No API costs.

How is that even possible? Two tricks. First, the model gets compressed to 2-bit weights (think of it as shrinking a huge file without losing much quality), which squeezes it down to fit in 128GB of RAM. Second, instead of keeping everything in memory, the conversation history gets saved to your SSD, so long sessions stay fast without eating your RAM.

Link: https://t.co/xBFSO2CGO3

items.reasoning: Highly noteworthy: a 284B model running locally on consumer hardware. Created by a legendary engineer (antirez/Redis). DeepSeek V4 Flash local inference represents a major accessibility milestone. Garry Tan amplification adds credibility.

items.title:
/5 Elon Musk open-sources X's feed ranking algorithm on GitHub.

X just open-sourced the full code b...
items.content:
/5 Elon Musk open-sources X's feed ranking algorithm on GitHub.

X just open-sourced the full code behind your For You feed. Not a blog post. Not a diagram. The actual production code, live on GitHub at xai-org/x-algorithm under an Apache 2.0 license, meaning you can use and modify it commercially.

Here is what the system actually does. Every time you open X, it pulls from 500 million daily posts, narrows them to roughly 1,500 candidates, and ranks them in under 200 milliseconds using a Grok-powered AI model.

Link: https://t.co/3bSGPdx8ff

items.title:
/2 NVIDIA-backed sparsity trick makes LLM training and inference 20% faster on H100s.

AI models alr...
items.content:
/2 NVIDIA-backed sparsity trick makes LLM training and inference 20% faster on H100s.

AI models already do less math than you'd think. Over 95% of neurons stay silent for any given word processed. That's free efficiency, right? Not quite.

The problem: GPUs hate irregular work. They're built for dense, predictable math. So when a model skips most of its calculations (called sparsity), the GPU still stumbles around looking for data in random memory spots, and actually runs slower.

Sakana AI and NVIDIA just fixed that. They built TwELL, a new data format that reshapes how sparse data is stored so it fits neatly into how GPUs already work. It routes 99% of sparse tokens through a fast path, with a dense backup for the rare heavy ones.

Link: https://t.co/YXB2fUEmDa
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: NVIDIA and Sakana AI developed TwELL, a data format that makes sparse LLM computation actually faster on GPUs by routing 99% of sparse tokens through an optimized fast path, achieving 20% speedup on H100s
items.reasoning: Practical hardware-level optimization from NVIDIA partnership that addresses a real bottleneck (GPUs being inefficient with sparse computation). 20% inference/training speedup on H100s is commercially significant.
items.summary_html: NVIDIA and Sakana AI developed TwELL, a data format that makes sparse LLM computation actually faster on GPUs by routing 99% of sparse tokens through an optimized fast path, achieving 20% speedup on H100s

items.content_html:
I met with a team last week. My job was to talk about Claude Code.

These are smart engineers who have been in tech for decades, building software.

They still left that room impressed by the state-of-the-art in agentic coding.

99% of people who aren't chronically online have no idea of what's going on.

items.title: Thanks to the @irfansenercom (a resident in @HackerResidency ) for teaching me how to vibe code iOS ...
items.content:
Thanks to the @irfansenercom (a resident in @HackerResidency ) for teaching me how to vibe code iOS apps with my mac mini, I now have the first mobile app fully created by claude.

It's a simple flight tracker app I build for fun and to learn the process, scan your boarding pass, use on-device AI to read the info, save into database, fully offline and private with no network access, and visualize your flight history into a 3d globe 🌎 

Native iOS app, swift UI, I never read a single line of code, all designs are created with GPT Image 2, app store listing and marketing copy are also creates by claude.

Took me a week talking to claude via telegram in my fee time.

More fun apps soon! 😁

items.title:
I love AI, it’s pure LLMs I hate.

Pure LLMs *are* basically just autocomplete.  

Recent progress (...
items.content:
I love AI, it’s pure LLMs I hate.

Pure LLMs *are* basically just autocomplete.  

Recent progress (e.g. Claude Code) doesn’t show otherwise

Rather, lot of the progress in the last two years has come from *introducing* other things – mainly classic symbolic techniques and tools, to offset the weaknesses of pure LLMs.

Shame to see the tweet below muddle all of this. 

If we want to make further progress we need to understand where the progress is coming from; mostly it is coming from leaving pure LLMs behind.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus's main thesis post: loves AI but hates pure LLMs; argues LLMs are just autocomplete; recent progress (Claude Code etc.) comes from introducing symbolic techniques and tools, not LLM improvements; says further progress requires leaving pure LLMs behind
items.reasoning: Very high engagement (44K views, 472 likes), comprehensive statement of influential AI critic's position, substantive technical argument about source of AI progress, relevant to understanding current AI development trajectory

I love AI, it’s pure LLMs I hate.

Pure LLMs *are* basically just autocomplete.

Recent progress (e.g. Claude Code) doesn’t show otherwise

Rather, lot of the progress in the last two years has come from *introducing* other things – mainly classic symbolic techniques and tools, to offset the weaknesses of pure LLMs.

Shame to see the tweet below muddle all of this.

If we want to make further progress we need to understand where the progress is coming from; mostly it is coming from leaving pure LLMs behind.

items.title: The pure LLM debate - which I had for many years, here and elsewhere - is indeed no longer relevant....
items.content:
The pure LLM debate - which I had for many years, here and elsewhere - is indeed no longer relevant. Why? 

Because I won; nobody uses pure LLMs anymore. 

Nowadays all deployed objects are neurosymbolic, which was exactly the point of my infamous 2022 paper, Deep Learning is Hitting a Wall.

If you don’t know I won, it’s because you read the title and not the paper 🤷‍♂️

items.reasoning: High engagement (20K views, 144 likes), core thesis statement from prominent AI critic, substantive claim about industry direction that is largely accurate - modern AI systems do use extensive tooling and symbolic components

items.title: Full prompt (wrote this very quickly, w/ AI helping for time savings (hence the messiness)... this c...
items.content:
Full prompt (wrote this very quickly, w/ AI helping for time savings (hence the messiness)... this could be significantly better with just a little bit of work):

You are building a polished, playable first-person time-bending shooter prototype inspired by the provided reference image’s broad visual language: stark white low-poly city/interior space, red faceted humanoid enemies, black angular first-person weapon, cool cyan-white lighting, hard shadows, sparse HUD, and mobile-playable controls.

You have the reference image attached. Work autonomously. Use sub-agents heavily. Do not stop at a plan. Build, screenshot, verify, iterate, and only finish when the visual style is genuinely close.

Primary goal:
The final game must look like a professional low-poly white/red/black time-bending shooter, not a generic Three.js demo. Characters must be faceted red crystalline humanoids with correct proportions and silhouettes. Environment must be sparse, architectural, white/cyan, high-key, with angular cars/walls/vents/props. Weapon must be a chunky black faceted pistol in the foreground. Camera/framing must resemble the reference: first-person gun on right, enemies center-midground, white environment with strong depth.

Process requirement:
1. First build a verifier before building the game.
2. The verifier compares two images:
   - reference screenshot
   - current game screenshot
3. It must not be a pixel-perfect diff. It must judge semantic/style similarity: same visual family, enemy silhouette, low-poly faceting, palette, lighting, weapon shape, environment composition, camera framing, and overall believability.
4. The verifier must return a 0-100 score and structured feedback.
5. A score below 95 is failing. Also fail if any critical category is below 90.
6. Validate the verifier itself before trusting it:
   - reference vs reference should score 99-100
   - blank/unstyled/basic Three.js scene should score very low
   - a merely color-matched but stylistically wrong scene should fail

Build this verifier in `tools/style-verifier/`.

The verifier should combine:
- A Codex vision judge invoked programmatically with `codex exec`
- Structured JSON output using `--output-schema`
- Deterministic image checks where useful: palette proportions, red enemy area, white/cyan background dominance, foreground dark weapon region, composition regions, brightness/contrast
- A final weighted score

Suggested verifier invocation inside scripts:

```bash
codex exec \
  -m gpt-5.5 \
  -s read-only \
  -a never \
  --image "$REFERENCE_IMAGE" \
  --image "$CANDIDATE_IMAGE" \
  --output-schema tools/style-verifier/style-score.schema.json \
  - < tools/style-verifier/judge-prompt.md
```

Verifier JSON schema should include:

```json
{
  "overall": 0,
  "pass": false,
  "category_scores": {
    "enemy_silhouette_and_faceting": 0,
    "enemy_material_and_red_color": 0,
    "weapon_silhouette_and_position": 0,
    "environment_geometry": 0,
    "palette_and_lighting": 0,
    "camera_framing_and_composition": 0,
    "low_poly_consistency": 0,
    "same_game_feel": 0
  },
  "blocking_differences": [],
  "highest_impact_fixes": []
}
```

After the verifier passes its own sanity tests, build the visual scene only. Do not implement gameplay yet.

Use Three.js or another real 3D renderer. Do not use the reference image as a background, overlay, texture, or hidden cheat. The scene must be actual 3D geometry.

Sub-agent plan:
- Verifier agent: builds and validates `tools/style-verifier`.
- Reference analyst agent: writes a concrete visual spec from the reference: palette, proportions, enemy anatomy, weapon shape, environment layout, lighting, camera.
- Enemy modeling agent: creates red faceted humanoid geometry with correct blocky crystalline look.
- Weapon modeling agent: creates right-side black angular pistol with large barrel and faceted highlights.
- Environment agent: creates white/cyan angular city/interior space with cars, vents, wall blocks, floor grid, hard shadows.
- Lighting/postprocess agent: tunes bloom, color grading, fog, exposure, shadows, and outlines.
- Validator agent: repeatedly screenshots and runs verifier, then gives concrete fix instructions.

Iteration loop:
1. Start local dev server.
2. Capture desktop screenshot at 1152x648 and mobile screenshot.
3. Run verifier against the reference.
4. If overall <95 or any critical category <90, identify the lowest categories.
5. Spawn focused sub-agents to fix those categories.
6. Rebuild and screenshot again.
7. Repeat until score >=95 and the validator agrees the scene looks like the same visual family.
8. Save every iteration screenshot and verifier JSON under `artifacts/visual-iterations/`.

Only after visual style passes:
- Implement gameplay.
- First-person movement.
- Mobile touch controls.
- Tap/click shooting.
- Time moves mostly when the player moves/aims/shoots.
- Red enemies shatter into angular fragments when hit.
- Enemy projectiles or attacks.
- Restart/win/lose flow.
- Minimal HUD.
- Keep visual style intact after gameplay additions.

Final verification:
- Run tests/build.
- Run Playwright screenshots on desktop and phone viewport.
- Run style verifier again after gameplay is complete.
- Do not finish unless score is still >=95.
- Deploy to Railway only after local verification passes.
- Return the Railway URL, final verifier score, and paths to the final screenshots and verifier report.

Acceptance criteria:
- Real 3D scene, not screenshot overlay.
- Looks visually cohesive and professional.
- Red enemies are faceted humanoids, not simple red capsules or mannequins.
- Weapon reads as a black angular first-person pistol.
- White/cyan environment has depth, geometry, cars/blocks/vents/props, and hard shadows.
- Mobile playable.
- Style verifier passes with >=95.
```

This should force the next run to treat visual similarity as the first deliverable, instead of building gameplay first and trying to style it afterward.

Now, please release this for Codex.

items.title:
Harnesses in AI: A Deep Dive

@TejasKumar_  builds a browser agent on GPT-3.5 Turbo that has one job...
items.content:
Harnesses in AI: A Deep Dive

@TejasKumar_  builds a browser agent on GPT-3.5 Turbo that has one job: upvote a post on Hacker News.

Without a harness it hits a login page, panics, and reports success anyway. The upvote never happened.

https://t.co/VgUEThloJX

He fixes it without touching the prompt once. Guardrails cap the iteration count and compact context when it bloats. A verify step reads the actual tool call history to catch the lie. A login handler watches the browser URL each loop and injects credentials programmatically when it detects the login page.

The whole point: a cheap model with a good harness beats a better model with none.
items.author: @aiDotEngineer
items.summary: AI Engineer shares detailed breakdown of using harnesses (guardrails, verification, error handling) to make cheap AI models outperform expensive ones in agentic tasks
items.reasoning: Highly relevant practical AI engineering insight: cheap model + good harness > expensive model without one. Shows browser agent failing without guardrails. Good engagement (106 likes, 4.9K views), actionable technical content

items.title: Most work on diffusion-based language generation has settled on operating directly over discrete tok...
items.content:
Most work on diffusion-based language generation has settled on operating directly over discrete tokens, partly because earlier attempts to do generation in a continuous space underperformed, and it was unclear whether that gap came from language being fundamentally discrete or from design choices that had not been explored carefully.

This 2026 paper from MIT maps tokens into a continuous embedding space using a frozen pretrained encoder, then learns to turn Gaussian noise into clean embeddings through a process called Flow Matching (a method that learns a velocity field describing how a noisy point should move, step by step, toward a clean one), and keeps the entire denoising trajectory in that continuous space, only converting back to actual words at the very last step using the same network that did the denoising.

Because the method stays continuous almost the whole way, established tricks from image generation—most notably classifier-free guidance (see this paper https://t.co/AzM6tX0UDA) carry over almost directly, which is awkward to do with discrete approaches.

The authors report that this design reaches better generation quality with far fewer sampling steps and roughly ten times less training data than competing discrete and continuous systems, without the extra distillation step that rivals often need to go fast, and they show it working on translation and summarization as well.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/bGhErOwg6Z

PDF: https://t.co/tb4Ep5P6oy
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes a 2026 MIT paper on continuous-space diffusion for language generation using Flow Matching, achieving better quality with fewer steps and less training data.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical paper summary about a potentially important architecture shift (continuous diffusion for text). Moderate engagement but high technical depth.
items.summary_html: Burkov summarizes a 2026 MIT paper on continuous-space diffusion for language generation using Flow Matching, achieving better quality with fewer steps and less training data.

Most work on diffusion-based language generation has settled on operating directly over discrete tokens, partly because earlier attempts to do generation in a continuous space underperformed, and it was unclear whether that gap came from language being fundamentally discrete or from design choices that had not been explored carefully.

This 2026 paper from MIT maps tokens into a continuous embedding space using a frozen pretrained encoder, then learns to turn Gaussian noise into clean embeddings through a process called Flow Matching (a method that learns a velocity field describing how a noisy point should move, step by step, toward a clean one), and keeps the entire denoising trajectory in that continuous space, only converting back to actual words at the very last step using the same network that did the denoising.

Because the method stays continuous almost the whole way, established tricks from image generation—most notably classifier-free guidance (see this paper https://t.co/AzM6tX0UDA) carry over almost directly, which is awkward to do with discrete approaches.

The authors report that this design reaches better generation quality with far fewer sampling steps and roughly ten times less training data than competing discrete and continuous systems, without the extra distillation step that rivals often need to go fast, and they show it working on translation and summarization as well.

items.title:
The "model weights are unreadable" excuse just died in one paper. 

Neural networks have billions of...
items.content:
The "model weights are unreadable" excuse just died in one paper. 

Neural networks have billions of parameters. 

Nobody really knows what each one does.
 
A new paper introduces adVersarial Parameter Decomposition. 

The method splits a model's weights into small, single-purpose subcomponents.
 
Each piece handles one specific job. 

Things like emoticon prediction or gender identification.
 
Only a tiny fraction fires on any given input.
 
How they pulled this off:
 
1. Break weights into simple parts
2. Ablate components adversarially during training
3. Keep only what preserves behavior
 
The breakthrough is attention. 

The technique decomposes attention computations even when spread across multiple heads.
 
That problem went unsolved for three years.
 
It scales beyond toy networks to real four-layer language models. 

The authors now consider it a serious competitor to sparse autoencoders.
 
You can trace information flow through attribution graphs. 

You can hand-edit specific behaviors and predict the outcome.
 
VPD makes weights legible.

items.title: There are a lot of coding and reasoning benchmarks for AI agents, but not a lot for document underst...
items.content:
There are a lot of coding and reasoning benchmarks for AI agents, but not a lot for document understanding - which is a prerequisite for all downstream knowledge work.

We released ParseBench ~a month ago, and it is one of the most comprehensive benchmarks that test whether frontier models can understand real-world enterprise documents.

This includes complex pages with dense tables, charts, layouts, and more. Most real-world documents around finance, insurance, and legal have one or more of these dimensions.

We're hosting a live webinar next Wednesday to talk about document understanding benchmarking, come check it out: https://t.co/q0LUviHqyN

You can access the full benchmark, paper, and leaderboards through our main site here:  https://t.co/PWczfhp0OX

items.title: A model can describe a video of a room fluently, but does it actually hold a mental picture of where...
items.content:
A model can describe a video of a room fluently, but does it actually hold a mental picture of where things are and how far apart they sit?

The authors  of this CVPR 2025 Best Paper Candidate built VSI-Bench, a test of about 5,000 questions over roughly 290 real indoor videos, covering things like counting objects, judging which item is closest to another, estimating distances and room sizes, and recalling the order objects appeared.

They ran fifteen models through it and found that the best ones land well below people (about 79% for humans versus the mid-40s for the strongest model), but not at random either, so some spatial ability is present.

Asking the model to "think step by step," a prompting trick that usually helps with language and math, made spatial answers worse, not better, which points to spatial reasoning being a separate weakness rather than a reasoning-effort problem.

Furthermore, when the authors asked a model to sketch a rough grid map of object positions, it placed neighboring objects fairly well (around 64% correct) but degraded sharply for objects far apart, suggesting these models stitch together accurate local fragments without a single coherent map of the whole space; forcing the model to draw and then use such a map improved its distance answers.

Learn from the paper with an AI tutor: https://t.co/YlmRY8kNCX

PDF: https://t.co/kzvY6JzE2e
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov summarizes CVPR 2025 Best Paper Candidate VSI-Bench testing spatial reasoning in video models - finding models far below human performance with chain-of-thought making things worse.
items.reasoning: Important finding about fundamental limitations in spatial reasoning for vision-language models. Best paper candidate status adds credibility. CoT hurting spatial tasks is counterintuitive.
items.summary_html: Burkov summarizes CVPR 2025 Best Paper Candidate VSI-Bench testing spatial reasoning in video models - finding models far below human performance with chain-of-thought making things worse.

items.title: The standard way of measuring whether language models can do grade-school math may be misleading: GS...
items.content:
The standard way of measuring whether language models can do grade-school math may be misleading: GSM8K is a single fixed set of questions, so a high score could reflect memorization or contamination of the test data into training, and it gives no way to vary a question and see whether a model's competence holds up.

To get around this, the authors of this ICLR 2025 paper build GSM-Symbolic, where each original question becomes a template with named slots for people and numbers plus rules that keep the answer valid, so they can generate many versions of the same underlying problem and look at performance as a spread of scores rather than a single number.

Running this across more than twenty open and closed models, they find that scores swing noticeably just from changing names or values even though the reasoning needed stays identical, that the original GSM8K score usually sits at the high end of this spread (which fits the contamination worry), and that accuracy falls and becomes more erratic as more clauses are added to a problem.

Their sharpest result comes from GSM-NoOp, where they insert a sentence that sounds relevant but changes nothing about the math; models routinely fold that sentence into a calculation anyway, with drops as large as 65 percent, and giving them worked examples of the same question does not fix it, which points toward pattern matching rather than genuine step-by-step reasoning.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/x6e1elXV3K

PDF: https://t.co/7JxaOATyrA

items.title:
This is the case in many industries now I think

The quality of people's work is so low that you jus...
items.content:
This is the case in many industries now I think

The quality of people's work is so low that you just simply can't hire for most things anymore, hiring is a net negative because you pay people to do the things you want them to do but they will do them wrong

So hiring now is kinda like doing charity work because all you do is pay people lots of money + lots of tax, and then all you do is teach people stuff (training) but then when they're good they just leave anyway

But then at the same time AI isn't good enough to do most of these human things well (yet)

items.title: Modern reinforcement learning systems like Dreamer train policies entirely on imagined experience: a...
items.content:
Modern reinforcement learning systems like Dreamer train policies entirely on imagined experience: a learned model predicts what the environment would do, a second learned model guesses what reward each imagined step would earn, and the policy improves from these fabricated trajectories without ever touching the real environment during the update.

When you train a policy on imagined experience, the policy ends up worse than if you'd trained it in the real world. The gap between "how good the policy looks in imagination" and "how good it actually is" comes from two separate mistakes: the dynamics model predicting the wrong next state, and the reward model predicting the wrong reward. Earlier theory mixed these together. This paper by @ylecun and colleagues pulls them apart, so you can see exactly how much of the damage comes from each one, and how much each can be controlled on its own.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/E9lm0D6UK5

PDF: https://t.co/6WLc7aqB3u

items.title: Latent diffusion image generators are built from two trained components: a compression model and a g...
items.content:
Latent diffusion image generators are built from two trained components: a compression model and a generator.

The compression model is trained on its own to take a picture, reduce it to a small set of numbers, and rebuild the original from those numbers as faithfully as possible; the generator then learns to produce pictures by producing those small numerical codes rather than raw pixels, and only its codes get expanded back into images, which is what keeps the computation manageable since a code can be hundreds of times smaller than the pixel grid.

The size of that code matters: the usual setup shrinks each image dimension by a factor of eight, but as target images get larger even that is not aggressive enough, and squeezing harder tends to destroy fine detail (text especially) while also leaving the code's statistics messy enough that the generator struggles to learn from them.

This May 2026 work from the Qwen team builds a family of compression models that shrink each dimension by sixteen or thirty-two times while keeping reconstruction sharp, combining three ideas.

The thirty-two-times model reaches text fidelity beating several sixteen-times competitors, and their sixteen-times model exceeds the standard eight-times baselines, which they report as the first result of its kind.

ChapterPal: https://t.co/7AiF4zAdKB

PDF: https://t.co/Ak1NoKZz0S

items.summary: Burkov summarizes May 2026 Qwen team paper on improved latent diffusion compression models achieving 16x and 32x compression while maintaining quality.

items.title: This ICLR 2025 paper documents OpenHands, a software platform that lets an AI agent operate a comput...
items.content:
This ICLR 2025 paper documents OpenHands, a software platform that lets an AI agent operate a computer the way a developer does: writing and running code, issuing shell commands, and navigating web pages inside an isolated container.

The technical core is an event stream, which is simply a running log of every action the agent takes and every observation it gets back; the agent reads this history at each step and decides what to do next, so building a new agent reduces to writing one function that maps the current history to the next action.

Rather than giving the agent a fixed menu of tools, the design lets it express any action as ordinary Python or bash, which means new capabilities can be added as plain Python functions instead of being baked into the framework.

The authors also wire in fifteen existing evaluation benchmarks covering bug fixing, real GitHub issue resolution, web navigation, and tool use, and report how the same generalist agent does across all of them without per-task tuning.

The paper provides a concrete, implementation-level picture of how a code-executing agent is actually put together, including the parts usually left vague: how execution is sandboxed, how one agent hands a subtask to another, and how the team keeps agent behavior from silently regressing by recording and replaying model responses as deterministic tests.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/9fyKXu9fQL

PDF: https://t.co/CnnX565DbX

items.title: Most production systems that do retrieval or classification still run on BERT, a model released in 2...
items.content:
Most production systems that do retrieval or classification still run on BERT, a model released in 2018, even though the techniques used to build language models have changed substantially since then; the encoder family (models that read text and produce representations, rather than generating new text the way GPT-style models do) was largely left behind while attention went to large generative models. 

The authors take the accumulated improvements from recent generative models—rotary positional embeddings (a way of encoding word order that extends cleanly to longer texts), gated activation functions, alternating local and global attention so most layers only look at nearby words while a few look at everything, and removal of padding tokens that waste computation—and apply them to encoder models, then train on 2 trillion tokens including code at a native context length of 8192 tokens (sixteen times BERT's 512-word limit).

The resulting models, ModernBERT-base and -large, do better than prior encoders across classification, single- and multi-vector retrieval, and code search, while processing long inputs roughly twice as fast as the next fastest competitor.

Since its December 2024 release, ModernBERT has become a widely adopted encoder backbone, with the approach extended into multilingual variants and broadly treated as the current default starting point for encoder-based retrieval and classification work.

Read and learn with an AI tutor: https://t.co/Rw5VuqKTFd

PDF: https://t.co/6jtec7m5YM

items.summary: Burkov explains ModernBERT paper - applying modern LLM techniques to encoder models, achieving 2x speed and better performance than BERT across tasks.
items.reasoning: Good technical summary of an important development in encoder models. ModernBERT becoming the new default for retrieval/classification is significant for production systems.

items.title:

I call it 😎 Vibe Walk

Unlike Google Maps, ...
items.content:
Really rough draft of trying to add routing to Hoodmaps

I call it 😎 Vibe Walk

Unlike Google Maps, Hoodmaps will route you away from and around crime areas and if possible also avoiding tourist areas

In this case it successfully avoids the crime area in Lisbon but lets you go a bit through tourist because otherwise it'd be too far around

I will add this to the main Hoodmaps soon if it works well enough!

items.content:
@thirdpairdemon Haha.
From 2015 to 2024, the number of PhD graduates in the US went from about 55,000 to about 55,000.
In the same period, the number of PhD graduates in China went from about 50,000 to about 100,000.

The number of federally-funded PhD fellowships in the US will go down by a factor of 4 if Congress doesn't change Trump’s budget proposal.

items.title:
/3 Open-source tool turns any home WiFi router into a through-wall human radar.

RuView just hit 50,...
items.content:
/3 Open-source tool turns any home WiFi router into a through-wall human radar.

RuView just hit 50,000+ GitHub stars, and the concept is genuinely wild: your WiFi router is already bouncing radio waves around every room. When a person moves or breathes, those waves scatter differently. 

RuView reads those tiny disturbances and figures out what's happening inside a room, through walls, no cameras needed.

To try it without any hardware, just run the Docker demo. For real sensing, you need ESP32-S3 boards, around $9 each.

Link: https://t.co/UdFMcXSTvP

@justin_xyz Almost everything Chinese tech is superior to Japan right now but you're not ready to have that conversation due to politics

Chinese trains 450km/h > Japanese trains 320km/h
Chinese EVs > Japanese EVs (just 2% of their output)
Chinese batteries (70% of market) > Japanese batteries 
Chinese drones  > Japanese drones (do they even exist?)
Chinese ecom (Taobao, JD, etc) > Japanese ecom (Rakuten the dinosaur)
Chinese AI (DeepSeek, Qwen, Kimi, Minimax etc) > Japanese AI (does it even have AI, ah yes that Xperia AI filter right, right?)
Chinese coffee (Luckin is bigger than Starbucks now and tastes better) > Japanese coffee is great to be fair, artesanal, and also $35 insanely expensive

items.title: Note that as of last summer Waymo said that LLMs/VLMs were experimental, and that their foundation m...
items.content:
Note that as of last summer Waymo said that LLMs/VLMs were experimental, and that their foundation model then could  “process only a small amount of image frames, does not incorporate accurate 3D sensing modalities like LiDAR or radar and is computationally expensive”

If Waymo has replaced their mostly (?) symbolic model with LLMs/VLMs since then (something that is not publicly disclosed) it’s not obvious that it has made a difference. 

Certainly Waymo Fall 2025, which was not primarily LLM or VLM based, was not “in the stone age”, by any reasonable measure.

@bindureddy this is a such a muddle. (at least relative to my views)

LLMs are more or less just autcomplete, but (as I have always said) they have their uses.

And the real progress now is coming from adding new (symbolic) techniques to the mix, not from pure scaling.

items.title: After about a year of adrenaline rush with vibe coding, I'm satisfied with what @ChapterPal has beco...
items.content:
After about a year of adrenaline rush with vibe coding, I'm satisfied with what @ChapterPal has become as a platform. It has a core of loyal and motivated paid users and steady userbase growth.

I'm back to writing The Hundred-Page Reinforcement Learning Book. I already feel that this will be the most approachable book on RL, a topic that most are afraid of because objectively it's much harder compared to supervised learning.

Just look at these two illustrations. The left one shows gradient ascent with the true gradient. The right one shows what changes when we replace the true gradient with its unbiased estimate. The concept of an unbiased estimate of the gradient is central to all policy gradient algorithms (including PPO and GRPO used to train LLMs), so getting it correctly early on is not optional.

As usual, I don't have a job other than being an author, so if you are awaiting this book, I invite you to consider supporting me by becoming a Cool Supporter 🚀 on Substack or Patreon. The details are here: https://t.co/1Bj1wAzTLL

What does it take to lead securely in an AI-driven world?

87% of CFOs think they’re secure. Most aren’t.

Security = foundation for AI, not a blocker.

items.content:
@Christiano92 1. read the list of issues discussed here: https://t.co/PcP5F7sxU0
and 
2. Know that the New York Times fact checked my understanding of this with *Waymo* in October 2025 and Waymo said they had no issues.

I am not going to believe an anon without strong sources over that, sorry. 

And yes I do agree that ML has been in used in many aspects throughout. But as of October 2025 Waymo’s worked mostly fine in a modular neurosymbolic fashion with LLMs etc only as components; not end-to-end.  Waymo confirmed this.

Good day to you, unless you can *source* your statements.

My biggest takeaway:

We are moving from recording reality to reconstructing it.

That is a major pl...
items.content:
My biggest takeaway:

We are moving from recording reality to reconstructing it.

That is a major platform shift, and the companies that understand it early will have an edge in how they train, build, and analyze.

Watch the full video to see what this looks like in practice, and check out what InSpatio is doing with InSpatio World.

Where do you think navigable video becomes most valuable first, robotics, gaming, training, or real-world analysis?

items.title: @GrizzledTexan @ChapterPal No, I didn't have a spec in the sense of one I would continuously keep up...

items.summary_html: Burkov sharing his LLM-based development workflow - writing detailed prompts and asking for clarifying questions rather than maintaining specs.
items.content_html:
@GrizzledTexan @ChapterPal No, I didn't have a spec in the sense of one I would continuously keep up to date.

For a complex request, I would write a prompt with all the details and constraints then ask the LLM to ask me clarifying questions before starting coding.

But for most of the incremental changes I simply said what I need to be changed and what is the expected result and that's it.

What got my attention is that time becomes flexible too.

You can:

→ move forward
→ rewind
→ return...
items.content:
What got my attention is that time becomes flexible too.

You can:

→ move forward
→ rewind
→ return to a moment and analyze it from another angle

That changes the use case entirely.

This is not passive content.

It is interactive spatial intelligence.

For executives, that opens up serious implications for:

→ robotics training
→ immersive game creation
→ real-world event analysis
→ simulation-driven decision making

items.summary: Ronald van Loon elaborates on time-flexible interactive spatial intelligence for robotics training, gaming, and simulation
items.reasoning: Continuation of spatial AI thread with specific use cases articulated
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon elaborates on time-flexible interactive spatial intelligence for robotics training, gaming, and simulation
items.content_html:
What got my attention is that time becomes flexible too.

You can:

→ move forward

→ rewind

→ return to a moment and analyze it from another angle

That changes the use case entirely.

This is not passive content.

It is interactive spatial intelligence.

For executives, that opens up serious implications for:

→ robotics training

→ immersive game creation

→ real-world event analysis

→ simulation-driven decision making

items.title:
The breakthrough is not just better video generation.

It is a new interface to reality.

Instead of...
items.content:
The breakthrough is not just better video generation.

It is a new interface to reality.

Instead of being locked into one camera angle, you can:

→ move across viewpoints
→ inspect scenes from new perspectives
→ revisit the same moment from a different position

That means the value is no longer in the clip alone.

It is in the world model behind it.

items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus defends his neurosymbolic position regarding Waymo - you need both neural and symbolic for optimal function; notes NYT fact-checked his description with Waymo who confirmed it
items.reasoning: Part of substantive technical debate about symbolic+neural approaches in production systems
items.summary_html: Marcus defends his neurosymbolic position regarding Waymo - you need both neural and symbolic for optimal function; notes NYT fact-checked his description with Waymo who confirmed it
items.content_html:
@Christiano92 you need both for optimal function; that’s the claim i have been making for 30 years. neuro+symbolic.

as a point of fact though, most of waymo’s LLM stuff at least as of last summer was still experimental. i am not actually sure how much lift they are getting from LLMs in production, though they are actively trying them. but they had them running for years without any LLM in production.

i do know that the New York Times fact checked my description of their tech in my October oped that mentioned Waymo and that Waymo had no objections.

items.title:
At Microsoft when anyone met with Bill Gates we prepared for weeks.

Why? The time value of his time...
items.content:
At Microsoft when anyone met with Bill Gates we prepared for weeks.

Why? The time value of his time was more than most of us made in a year.

I didn’t realize an hour could have so much impact. Elon explains it here. 

Tonight I took my friend to Big Sur for sunset.

It only lasts a few minutes but is similarly impactful.

You have to plan for that too. Grok will tell you when sunset is in any location.

We considered Lake Tahoe but decided against it.

I tell you all this because the best moments are planned. Because they are too valuable.

Separately I asked Bill for a billion dollars.

Hey you gotta take the shot. You don’t score goals by not trying.

If I walked into a meeting with @elonmusk I would start with “the next hour will be worth way more than $100 million to you.”

That isn’t true. I waste his time now by asking for a @neuralink. Or am I? I would use it to make him a lot more than that. One human could run 1,000 robots another robot entrepreneur told me with one on that is coming in next decade.

@paulg calls them “highly leverageable moments.”

Hope you experience a few in your lives.

They are what you will tell the grandkids about.

Oh and the second part is trying to figure out the universe. When you start really studying the sun it is crazy. A constant radiation explosion.

So many are dreaming about harvesting its energy in space.

So maybe a sunset viewing is entrepreneurial behavior? And highly leverage in other ways. Talked to cofounder of Robinhood about that. 

I asked him for keys to his car. Why not? :-) https://t.co/0JgMFOE5NH

items.title:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLea...
items.content:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

This is the type of application I've already built earlier at the beginning of this year for a conference demonstration! 

GPT Paper-Reader, to show how you can leverage the summarization capabilities of large language models to retrieve research papers from databases, summarize their content, and engage in Q&A interactions with people.    The future is here.  In addition to the regular theoretical presentation, I'll be presenting a practical GPT. This application utilizes GPT, to effectively read and analyze complete academic papers:  Basically the app chunks the PDF paper into manageable sections for detailed reading and generates concise summaries for each segment. By maintaining context from previous sections within the token limit, it enhances comprehension. Before diving into the paper, you can outline specific questions in the prompt. This approach allows GPT to extract the most important information during its reading and summarizing process, leading to superior outcomes. items.content_html:
RAG with LLM: Creating an AI-Powered File Reader!

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode

This is the type of application I've already built earlier at the beginning of this year for a conference demonstration!

GPT Paper-Reader, to show how you can leverage the summarization capabilities of large language models to retrieve research papers from databases, summarize their content, and engage in Q&A interactions with people. The future is here. In addition to the regular theoretical presentation, I'll be presenting a practical GPT. This application utilizes GPT, to effectively read and analyze complete academic papers: Basically the app chunks the PDF paper into manageable sections for detailed reading and generates concise summaries for each segment. By maintaining context from previous sections within the token limit, it enhances comprehension. Before diving into the paper, you can outline specific questions in the prompt. This approach allows GPT to extract the most important information during its reading and summarizing process, leading to superior outcomes. After summarizing all parts, you will receive comprehensive answers to your inquiries based on the consolidated summaries. By default, the initialized prompt will target essential points such as: These inquiries are tailored for research articles within the computer science domain. Upon completion of the paper review, feel free to engage with the question() interface to ask further questions.

items.title: People outside of academia have no idea how far left that whole institution has drifted. These are n...
items.content:
People outside of academia have no idea how far left that whole institution has drifted. These are not your average left-wing coworkers in other professional domains. I know a lot of old professors who used to be typical 1960s "radicals" who are downright conservative by the standards of most college professors today. 

Years ago I decided that I stood no chance of any career advancement in that political environment and made a long painful sojourn into a career change. When I first started working for some tech startups headquartered in places like LA and SF, staffed to the brim with California liberals, I was pleasantly shocked how much more normal those working environments were.
items.author: @tunguz

People outside of academia have no idea how far left that whole institution has drifted. These are not your average left-wing coworkers in other professional domains. I know a lot of old professors who used to be typical 1960s "radicals" who are downright conservative by the standards of most college professors today.

Years ago I decided that I stood no chance of any career advancement in that political environment and made a long painful sojourn into a career change. When I first started working for some tech startups headquartered in places like LA and SF, staffed to the brim with California liberals, I was pleasantly shocked how much more normal those working environments were.

items.title:
Another thing I learnt is

I always hated tourist areas for good reason, especially after living in ...
items.content:
Another thing I learnt is

I always hated tourist areas for good reason, especially after living in Amsterdam for almost a decade as a Dutch person and we'd generally avoid the center because it was such a tourist dump, and very few things that the foreigners there did had much to do with the things we as Dutch people did which presumably is what they came here for, Dutch culture right?

Realizing that's the same in most countries I liked to travel and try find the somewhat local areas outside the tourist zones, I just wanted to live like a local (as pretentious as that sounds) 10 years ago that was fun and I still try to!

What I never realized that yes the tourist areas are a net negative and as you see in the Europe of 2026, they easily turn into unliveable areas of criminals, junkies, drug cartels and generally low-lifes you don't wanna be near

Why? Because if a neighborhood slowly becomes only tourists and not locals, the businesses change to only cater for tourists, the quality goes down because tourists are transient and businesses won't care if their customers have a bad experience, they have a fresh supply of tourists on Thursday and Friday they can rip off

Essentially the soul of a city dies in tourist districts, and with it any care and love to maintain the area's upkeep, and then you get shady people setting up shop there

It's a rotten ecosystem of tourists who think they are experiencing something local while not and the lowest tier bottom feeders of society preying on them to make money by ripping them off or as you see now just robbing them, it's a net negative

A good neighborhood is a healthy mix of majority locals, some foreigners and some tourists, once that % goes off balance, things go to shit fast as you can see in parts of Barcelona and other European cities

How to enforce that though I am not sure either

One thing I know is that the hipster (I guess now called trendy/artsy/cool, yellow on Hoodmaps) areas are usually a nice balance of that and are nice to live to for both locals, foreigners and fun to visit for tourists
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio writes a long analysis about why tourist districts degrade, arguing that when locals leave a neighborhood, quality drops and criminal elements move in.
items.reasoning: High engagement (102K views) but entirely about urban tourism dynamics, not AI/ML related.

items.content:
@FlintCasey Some of my credentials.

Siri was launched in my house. First consumer app to use AI.

Insta360 AI company was launched in my house. First camera to use AI.

I had the first ride in the first Mercedes AI car. And first ride in first Tesla. And first ride in BMW i3. And first ride in Fiat 500. And early rides in MANY others. 

Studied automotive safety innovation before anyone else in Silicon Valley did because two of my friends were killed in car wrecks. 

You haven't given Tesla FSD a fair chance. At all. If you feel Waymo is better.

items.title:
@VictorTaelin @sriramk In an exponential curve, the derivative is proportional to the value.
In othe...
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: LeCun explains exponential growth mathematics - relative growth rate is constant, bank accounts double at constant periods
items.reasoning: Technical clarification about exponential curves, likely in context of AI progress debates
items.summary_html: LeCun explains exponential growth mathematics - relative growth rate is constant, bank accounts double at constant periods
items.content_html:
@VictorTaelin @sriramk In an exponential curve, the derivative is proportional to the value.

In other words, the *relative growth rate* is constant.

And by the way, a bank account with fixed interest rate is doubling at a constant period. Just not every month.

items.title:
No I don't think so

People travel to experience other cultures, but if the place you travel to isn'...
items.content:
No I don't think so

People travel to experience other cultures, but if the place you travel to isn't that culture anymore then what are you traveling to?

Globalism is good and I don't like nationalism, but I do like preserving cultures

So for a neigborhood (or city) to be healthy it needs mostly locals, some foreigners, some tourists

That's a healthy mix
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio discusses the balance between globalism and preserving local culture in neighborhoods.
items.reasoning: Off-topic cultural/travel commentary.
items.summary_html: Levelsio discusses the balance between globalism and preserving local culture in neighborhoods.
items.content_html:
No I don't think so

People travel to experience other cultures, but if the place you travel to isn't that culture anymore then what are you traveling to?

Globalism is good and I don't like nationalism, but I do like preserving cultures

So for a neigborhood (or city) to be healthy it needs mostly locals, some foreigners, some tourists

That's a healthy mix

items.title:
Avoid @geekompc like the plague.

They offer 30-day returns, but you need to request a Return Author...
items.content:
Avoid @geekompc like the plague.

They offer 30-day returns, but you need to request a Return Authorization from them.

It turns out, they are making it really hard for me to return this thing. Their support team keeps sending me tips to fix my issues, and in their latest email, they want me to record videos of the problems in having with the computer.

I called them, and their phone support people can’t do anything about it. They keep “escalating this”, but nothing happens.

I’m 🤏 close to go to my credit card and put a chargeback. 

They lost me as a client forever.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino complaining about Geekom making returns difficult
items.reasoning: Consumer complaint, not AI related. 16K views likely due to svpino's following.
items.summary_html: Svpino complaining about Geekom making returns difficult
